sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient is a 5-year-old niece's guardian who hurt her knee while running with her niece, who was learning to ride a bike without training wheels.
She twisted her knee in a "funky way" and reports pain on both sides of the knee, more so on the medial aspect.
She heard a pop at the time of injury and describes the pain as a throbbing 4/10 that has worsened over time.
She has taken Tylenol for pain relief, but it only provides temporary relief.
She has a history of an ulcer and is not able to take ibuprofen or other NSAIDs.
Past medical history: ulcer.
Review of systems: knee pain.
Current medications: Tylenol as needed.
Objective:
Vital signs: Not mentioned.
Physical exam: The patient has edema and effusion in the knee, moderate range of motion, pain on flexion and extension, and a negative Lachman's test and valgus and varus tests.
Test results: Normal X-ray of the left knee with no fractures or bony abnormalities.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a medial collateral ligament (MCL) strain, likely caused by twisting her knee while running with her niece.
Problem list: MCL strain; active.
Plan:
I prescribed tramadol 50mg twice daily for pain management.
I recommended the patient to use a knee immobilizer for the next week to prevent further injury and reduce inflammation.
I advised her to ice the knee 3-4 times a day for 20-30 minutes to reduce swelling.
I also instructed her to avoid running with her niece for the next 3 weeks to allow the knee to heal.
I expect the patient to be back to normal in about a month.
Follow-up:
I scheduled a follow-up appointment in a couple of days to check on the patient's progress.
